title: 10 best herbal creams for stomach cramps

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Chamomile contains apigenin, a flavonoid that has anti-inflammatory properties, which helps to reduce inflammation and relieve stomach cramp discomfort.
Antispasmodic effects: The essential oil in chamomile, bisabolol, has antispasmodic properties that help to relax muscle spasms and ease cramping in the stomach.

Soothing and calming effects: Chamomile's apigenin and flavonoids have a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to reduce anxiety and stress-related symptoms that can contribute to stomach cramps, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Matricaria chamomilla, also known as chamomile, creams helps with stomach cramps because of its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. The active compounds in chamomile, such as apigenin and luteolin, have been shown to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing spasms and cramping. The cream's topical application allows the active ingredients to penetrate deep into the skin, providing fast relief from stomach cramps. This natural remedy has been used for centuries to ease digestive discomfort, making it a popular choice for those seeking a gentle and effective solution.
remedy_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that helps with stomach cramps by acting as an anti-inflammatory agent, reducing pain and inflammation in the digestive tract.
Luteolin: A flavonoid that helps with stomach cramps by inhibiting the release of pain-causing neurotransmitters and reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Alpha-bisabolol: A sesquiterpene that helps with stomach cramps by exhibiting analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, providing relief from pain and discomfort in the digestive system.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in soothing and anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, which help to calm digestive issues and reduce pain.
Seeds: Contain apigenin and other flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory properties, which aid in relieving stomach cramps and other digestive discomforts.
Leaves: Rich in flavonoids and other compounds that have a soothing effect on the digestive system, helping to alleviate stomach cramps and other gastrointestinal issues.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 pound of matricaria chamomilla flowers in the early morning to ensure optimal potency.
Dry the flowers in a single layer at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours to remove excess moisture.
Steep 1 cup of dried flowers in 2 cups of carrier oil such as coconut or olive oil for 2 weeks.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth into a clean container and discard the solids immediately.
Whip 1 cup of beeswax and 1/2 cup of shea butter into the infused oil until a smooth consistency is achieved.

remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm's antispasmodic properties help to relax the smooth muscle in the digestive tract, reducing spasms and cramping associated with stomach cramps.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds found in lemon balm cream may help reduce inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms of stomach cramps such as bloating and discomfort.

Anxiolytic properties: Lemon balm's anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) effects can also contribute to its ability to alleviate stomach cramps by reducing stress and promoting relaxation, which can help ease muscle tension and spasms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, creams helps with stomach cramps because of its calming and soothing properties. The active compounds in Melissa officinalis, such as rosmarinic acid and melissic acid, have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, which help to relax the smooth muscle in the digestive tract and reduce spasms. This can provide quick relief from stomach cramps, bloating, and discomfort, promoting overall digestive health and well-being. Its natural anti-inflammatory properties also help to reduce inflammation and ease digestive discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation and pain associated with stomach cramps.
Luteolin: This flavonoid has anti-spasmodic effects, which can help relax the smooth muscles in the digestive tract and alleviate cramping.
Melissic acid: This triterpenoid saponin has anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic properties, which can help soothe and calm the digestive tract, reducing cramping and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are primarily used due to their high content of essential oils, which have anti-inflammatory and soothing properties that help alleviate stomach cramps.
Stems: They contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that contribute to their medicinal properties, making them useful in reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with stomach cramps.
Roots: Although not the most commonly used part, roots of Melissa officinalis are also employed in herbal remedies due to their ability to calm digestive issues and reduce spasms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 cup of melissa flowers on a sunny morning after the dew has dried completely.
Dry the harvested melissa flowers in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried melissa flowers in 2 cups of boiling water for 5 minutes.
Mix 1 tablespoon of the melissa infusion with 1 tablespoon of beeswax and 1 tablespoon of coconut oil.
Stir and heat the mixture until the beeswax is fully incorporated and the cream is smooth and creamy.

remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which helps to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of stomach cramps such as pain and discomfort.
Licoric acid: This flavonoid has antioxidant properties, which can help to protect the stomach lining from damage caused by stress, bacteria, or other factors that may contribute to stomach cramps.
Mucilage: The gel-like substance found in licorice root has a soothing and protective effect on the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, providing relief from stomach cramps and discomfort.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, creams helps with stomach cramps because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The root of the licorice plant contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation. This can provide relief from stomach cramps, bloating, and discomfort associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other digestive issues. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation of the stomach muscles, licorice creams can help to alleviate stomach cramps and promote overall digestive health.
remedy_constituents: Licorice Glycosides (specifically, Glycyrrhizin): These glycosides act as potent anti-inflammatory agents, helping to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain associated with stomach cramps.

Phenolic Acids (specifically, Isoflavones and Flavonoids): These compounds exhibit antioxidant properties, which help to protect the stomach lining from oxidative stress and reduce inflammation, thereby providing relief from stomach cramps.

Saponins (specifically, Triterpene Saponins): These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the smooth muscles in the stomach and alleviate cramping, making them an effective remedy for stomach cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make creams for stomach cramps due to their high content of glycyrrhizin, which has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.
Barks: The barks of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make creams for stomach cramps due to their ability to soothe digestive issues and reduce inflammation.
Seeds: The seeds of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make creams for stomach cramps due to their rich content of flavonoids and phenolic acids that help to reduce spasms and inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 100 grams of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots and grind them into fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Combine the powder with 10 grams of beeswax and 50 grams of sweet almond oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the beeswax melts completely.
Remove the mixture from heat and add 10 grams of distilled water and 5 grams of emulsifying wax, stirring until fully incorporated.
Pour the mixture into a clean container and allow it to cool and thicken for at least 30 minutes.

remedy_properties: Shogaol: Shogaol, a bioactive compound found in ginger, has potent anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties, which can help to alleviate stomach cramps by reducing inflammation and relaxing muscle spasms.

Gingerols: Gingerols, another set of bioactive compounds found in ginger, have been shown to possess antispasmodic and analgesic properties, making them effective in relieving stomach cramps by decreasing muscle contractions and pain perception.

Volatile oils: The volatile oils present in herbal ginger creams, such as [6]-gingerol and [10]-gingerol, have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help to soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation that may be contributing to stomach cramps.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, creams helps with stomach cramps because of its anti-inflammatory properties. This natural remedy has been used for centuries to alleviate nausea and discomfort associated with menstrual cramps, motion sickness, and digestive issues. remedy_constituents: Gingerols: These sesquiterpenoid compounds have analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation in the stomach muscles, providing relief from cramps.

Shogaols: Similar to gingerols, shogaols are also sesquiterpenoid compounds found in ginger, and they have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that help to soothe the stomach and alleviate cramps.

remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: The most commonly used part for making creams to alleviate stomach cramps due to its high concentration of gingerols and shogaols, which have anti-inflammatory properties.

Roots: Although less commonly used than the rhyzomes, ginger roots can be used to make creams for stomach cramps due to their stored gingerols and shogaols, which are released when the root is harvested or processed.
remedy_recipe: Combine 250 grams of shea butter and 150 grams of coconut oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture for 10 minutes at 60 degrees celsius or 140 degrees fahrenheit.
Add 50 grams of beeswax and stir until fully incorporated into the mixture.
Allow the mixture to cool and thicken for 30 minutes at room temperature.
Add 10 grams of zingiber officinale essential oil and blend until well combined.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Cornflower extract contains flavonoids and anthocyanins, which have anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps.

Antispasmodic properties: The phenolic compounds present in cornflower cream, such as rosmarinic acid, can help relax smooth muscles in the digestive system, reducing spasms and cramping associated with stomach pain.

Soothing and protective properties: Cornflower's mucilages and saponins create a soothing film on the skin and mucous membranes, providing relief from irritations and protecting the digestive tract from further damage.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Centaurea cyanus, also known as cornflower, creams helps with stomach cramps because it contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that soothe irritated muscles and calm the digestive system. remedy_recipe: Harvest 100 grams of dried flowers of centaurea cyanus by cutting them at dawn when dew is still present.
Infuse 50 grams of the harvested flowers in 500 milliliters of carrier oil for 2 weeks.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean glass container to separate solids from liquid.
Mix 10 grams of beeswax with the infused oil in a double boiler and heat until the wax melts.
Pour the melted wax mixture into a tin mold and allow it to cool and solidify at room temperature for 30 minutes.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Pot marigold creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids, carotenoids, and saponins, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms of stomach cramps.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh calendula officinalis flowers in the morning when petals are fully open and dry them completely in a warm place for 1-2 weeks.
Combine 1 part dried calendula officinalis flowers with 2 parts carrier oil such as jojoba oil in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture in a cool dark place for 2-4 weeks shaking the jar every day to infuse the oil.
Strain the oil through a cheesecloth into a clean glass bowl and discard the solids.
Mix the infused oil with beeswax and vitamin e oil in a double boiler over low heat until the beeswax melts and the mixture reaches a smooth consistency.

remedy_recipe: Harvest foeniculum vulgare leaves and flowers at dawn when the plant is at its highest essential oil content.
Dry the harvested foeniculum vulgare plant material in a low temperature oven at 50 degrees celsius for 30 minutes.
Combine 100 grams of dried foeniculum vulgare plant material with 1000 milliliters of carrier oil in a double boiler.
Steep the mixture at 60 degrees celsius for 4 hours to infuse the carrier oil with the herbal extract.
Strain and filter the infused oil mixture through cheesecloth and a coffee filter to obtain a clear herbal cream base.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 cup of fresh mentha x piperita leaves and flowers from a pesticide-free area for immediate use.
Combine the harvested mentha x piperita with 1/4 cup of distilled water in a saucepan and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to low and simmer for 10 minutes to release the mentha x piperita's essential oils.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth and discard the solids to obtain a mentha x piperita extract.
Mix 2 tablespoons of the mentha x piperita extract with 2 tablespoons of beeswax and 2 tablespoons of coconut oil to create a smooth cream.

remedy_recipe: Grate 2 to 3 grams of curcuma longa rhizome into a bowl to release its active compounds.

Heat the mixture over low heat for 20 to 30 minutes to facilitate oil infusion.
Strain the curcuma longa infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean bowl to separate solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 50g of dried sambucus nigra flowers in late summer when fully ripe and fragrant for best results.

Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth into a clean glass container discard the solids.
Mix 1 tablespoon of the infused sambucus nigra oil with 1 tablespoon of beeswax and 1 tablespoon of vitamin e oil in a double boiler.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh crataegus monogyna leaves and flowers in the morning after dew has dried ensuring optimal potency.
Dry the harvested plant material using a warm air dryer for 3-4 hours until crispy brittle texture achieved.
Combine one part dried crataegus monogyna with two parts coconut oil and two tablespoons of beeswax in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over medium heat stirring occasionally until melted and well combined then allow to cool slightly.
Pour the warm cream into sterilized glass jars leaving 1/4 inch headspace and seal immediately for storage.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh aloe vera leaves from your garden or store them in the refrigerator for later use.
Rinse the aloe vera gel inside the leaves with cold distilled water and drain well within 15 minutes.
Combine 20 grams of aloe vera gel with 10 grams of calendula flowers and 5 grams of chamomile flowers in a blender.
Blend the mixture on low speed for exactly 2 minutes to create a smooth paste taking approximately 5 minutes.
Mix the paste with 50 grams of coconut oil and 25 grams of beeswax in a double boiler heating it at 160 degrees fahrenheit for 30 minutes.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Purple coneflower contains anti-inflammatory compounds like triterpenoid saponins, which help reduce inflammation and alleviate abdominal pain associated with stomach cramps.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh echinacea purpurea flowers and leaves in the early morning or late afternoon when they are at peak potency and dry them immediately to prevent degradation.
Combine 200g of dried echinacea purpurea flowers and leaves with 400ml of high-proof vodka in a glass jar, allowing it to infuse for 6-8 weeks to extract the active compounds.
Strain the infused liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another glass jar, discarding the solids and retaining the liquid which will be used as the herbal extract.

Mix 100g of beeswax with 50g of coconut oil and heat them gently in a double boiler until they are fully incorporated and melted, then stir in the echinacea purpurea herbal extract to create the cream base.
Pour the cooled cream base into sterilized glass jars or containers and store them in a cool dark place for at least 24 hours before use, allowing the ingredients to mature and blend together.

remedy_properties: Antispasmodic property: Herbal broad-leaved lime creams contain compounds like flavonoids, terpenes, and phenolic acids that help to relax muscles and reduce spasms, providing relief from stomach cramps. This is because these compounds can bind to receptors in the gut wall, reducing muscle contractions and inflammation.

Antioxidant property: The antioxidants present in herbal broad-leaved lime creams, such as vitamin C, beta-carotene, and polyphenols, help to protect the stomach lining from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to stomach cramps. By reducing oxidative stress, these antioxidants may help to soothe and calm the stomach.

Anti-inflammatory property: Herbal broad-leaved lime creams contain compounds like flavonoids, limonoids, and phenolic acids that have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps. This is because these compounds can inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, reducing inflammation and swelling.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the broad-leaved lime plant are rich in alkaloids and flavonoids, which help to soothe digestive issues and reduce inflammation.
Barks: The barks of the broad-leaved lime plant contain tannins that can help to relax the muscles and reduce spasms, providing relief from stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: Gather dried tilia platyphyllos flowers and calendula petals in equal quantities weighing approximately 20g each.
Combine the dried herbs with 40ml of cold-pressed olive oil in a clean glass jar and stir well.
Allow the mixture to infuse for 4 weeks, shaking the jar daily, before straining and discarding the solids.
Transfer the infused oil to a double boiler and mix with 20g of beeswax and 10ml of vitamin e oil, heating until melted.
Remove from heat, pour into sterilized containers, and let cool and solidify for at least 30 minutes before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Plantain creams helps with stomach cramps because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe and calm irritated digestive tissues. The cream's active ingredients, such as allantoin and phenolic acids, work to reduce inflammation and ease discomfort associated with stomach cramps. Additionally, plantain has a gentle, non-irritating effect on the skin, allowing it to penetrate deeply and provide targeted relief for the digestive system, making it an effective natural remedy for alleviating stomach cramps and promoting overall digestive health.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Plantain creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can alleviate stomach cramp discomfort and pain.
Antispasmodic: The herb plantain has antispasmodic properties that help relax muscle spasms in the stomach and intestines, providing relief from cramping and discomfort.

Protective and soothing: Plantain cream's protective and soothing properties help protect the digestive tract lining, reducing irritation and inflammation, which can further contribute to stomach cramps and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the plantain plant are used in medicinal creams due to their anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce swelling and pain caused by stomach cramps.
Barks: The barks of the plantain plant contain compounds that have a soothing effect on the digestive system, making them effective in relieving stomach cramps and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh plantago major leaves, wash them thoroughly with cold water and dry them with clean towels.
Combine 20g of dried plantago major herb with 200ml of sweet almond oil in a small saucepan.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, to facilitate decarbonization and infusion.
Strain the oil through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean glass container, discarding the solids.
Mix the infused oil with 20g of beeswax and 10ml of coconut oil, heating gently until fully melted and combined.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Jesuit's bark creams helps with stomach cramps because it contains a natural compound called alkaloids, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These compounds work to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce inflammation, providing quick relief from cramping and discomfort. Additionally, the cream's cooling sensation can help soothe the stomach lining, reducing irritation and spasms that can cause cramps. As a result, Jesuit's bark creams offer natural and effective relief from stomach cramps and related discomfort.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Jesuit's bark creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and terpenoids, which help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating stomach cramps and discomfort.

Antispasmodic properties: The creams' active ingredients, such as alkaloids and glycosides, have antispasmodic effects, which means they can relax smooth muscle contractions in the stomach, reducing cramping and spasms.

Softpith-strengthening properties: Some herbal Jesuit's bark creams contain saponins that help to strengthen and protect the soft tissues lining the digestive tract, reducing irritation and inflammation that can contribute to stomach cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in alkaloids, which help to relax the muscles and reduce spasms associated with stomach cramps.
Barks: The barks contain a high concentration of quinine, an anti-inflammatory compound that soothes digestive issues and reduces pain caused by stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20g of dried cinchona officinalis bark and grind it into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Combine the ground powder with 100ml of coconut oil and 50ml of beeswax in a heat-resistant glass bowl, stirring occasionally over low heat for 30 minutes.
Heat the mixture to 60-70°c, then remove from heat and let it infuse for 1 hour before straining the liquid through cheesecloth into another container.
Add 20ml of sweet almond oil, 10ml of jojoba oil, and 5g of vitamin e oil to the strained liquid, stirring well to combine before pouring the mixture into sterilized jars.
Let the cream cool and thicken at room temperature for at least 2 hours before applying topically to the affected area as needed or storing in a cool dark place for up to 6 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Curly dock creams helps with stomach cramps because of its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. The cream, made from the leaves of the curly dock plant, contains antioxidants that help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief from cramping and discomfort. Additionally, the cream's mucilaginous texture coats and soothes the lining of the stomach, further calming the digestive system and alleviating cramps. This natural remedy provides gentle and effective relief for those suffering from stomach cramps.
remedy_properties: Astringent properties: Curly dock's astringent properties help to reduce inflammation and soothe digestive issues, including stomach cramps, by constricting blood vessels and reducing excess mucus production.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Curly dock contains anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and polyphenols, which help to reduce swelling and pain associated with stomach cramps, and also protect the digestive tract from further irritation.

Antioxidant activity: The antioxidant properties of curly dock creams help to neutralize free radicals in the digestive tract, reducing oxidative stress and inflammation that can contribute to stomach cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in tannins and flavonoids, which help soothe inflammation and reduce pain associated with stomach cramps.
Rhyzomes: Contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, making them effective in alleviating stomach cramp symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 cups of fresh rumex crispus leaves and stems, rinse them thoroughly with cool water.
Combine the herbs with 2 tablespoons of glycerin in a blender or food processor for 30 seconds.
Add 2 ounces of beeswax and 6 ounces of coconut oil to the mixture, blend until smooth.
Heat the mixture at 160°f for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, to create a homogeneous consistency.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass jar, let it cool completely.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Field horsetail creams helps with stomach cramps because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties. The cream contains saponins, a type of phytochemical that soothes irritated tissues and reduces inflammation in the digestive tract. When applied topically to the abdomen, it calms spasms and eases cramping pain caused by conditions like IBS, food poisoning, or menstrual cramps. Additionally, field horsetail's antimicrobial properties help eliminate any underlying infections that may be contributing to stomach discomfort.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Field Horsetail contains anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and saponins, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating stomach cramps caused by inflammation.

Antispasmodic properties: The herb has antispasmodic properties that can help relax smooth muscle contractions in the digestive system, thereby relieving spasms and cramps associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), Crohn's disease, and other conditions.

Antioxidant activity: Field Horsetail's antioxidant properties can help protect the digestive tract from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which may contribute to stomach cramps and other gastrointestinal issues.
remedy_parts: Roots: Contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe and calm stomach cramps by reducing inflammation and discomfort.
Bark: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids that help alleviate symptoms of stomach cramps, including bloating and digestive issues.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried equisetum arvense herb and grind it into fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 3 minutes.
Combine the ground equisetum arvense with 2 tablespoons of coconut oil in a small saucepan over low heat stirring constantly for 10 minutes.
Remove the saucepan from heat and let the mixture steep for 30 minutes to allow the herbal properties to infuse into the oil.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean glass bowl discarding the solids and allowing it to cool at room temperature for 2 hours.
Whip the cooled oil mixture with an electric mixer until it forms a smooth cream consistency before transferring it to airtight containers for storage and use within 6 weeks.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St john's wort creams helps with stomach cramps because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe irritated tissues in the digestive tract. The cream's active ingredients, such as hyperforin and hypericin, work to reduce inflammation and relax muscle spasms, providing quick relief from cramping discomfort. By calming the gut and reducing discomfort, St john's wort creams can help alleviate stomach cramps associated with IBS, menstrual periods, and other digestive issues, promoting a sense of calm and comfort.
remedy_properties: Hyperforin: Hyperforin, a bioactive compound found in St John's Wort, has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, helping to reduce pain and discomfort associated with stomach cramps.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids present in St John's Wort creams have antioxidant properties that help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by inflammation and oxidative stress, which can contribute to stomach cramps.
Tannins: Tannins, a type of polyphenol, have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, helping to relax smooth muscle contractions in the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramp symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help reduce inflammation and ease muscle spasms associated with stomach cramps.
Barks: Contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, making them effective in soothing digestive discomforts.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers for immediate use or drying for later preparation.
Dry the hypericum leaves and flowers in a cool dark place for at least 24 hours to preserve their active compounds.
Combine one part dried hypericum leaf and flower mixture with three parts coconut oil and heat gently until infused.
Strain the infusion through cheesecloth or a coffee filter to remove the herbal solids and discard them.
Mix the remaining infused oil with beeswax in a 1:1 ratio and melt together at low heat for about 10 minutes.

intro_desc: Herbal creams for stomach cramps are topical ointments infused with natural herbs that provide relief from abdominal pain and discomfort. They help by reducing inflammation and relaxing the muscles in the stomach. Examples include creams containing ginger, peppermint, and chamomile, which have been shown to alleviate stomach cramps and improve digestion. For instance, a pregnant woman experiencing stomach cramps can benefit from using a ginger-infused cream to reduce her discomfort and improve her overall well-being.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to stomach cramps that are treated with herbal creams are menstrual cramps, fibromyalgia, and arthritis. Herbal creams containing ingredients such as arnica, ginger, and capsaicin can help alleviate pain and reduce inflammation associated with these conditions. These natural remedies can provide relief without the side effects of over-the-counter medications, making them a popular alternative for many individuals seeking natural pain management solutions.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal creams that help with stomach cramps is a blend of peppermint, ginger, and chamomile. Peppermint cream cools and soothes the digestive tract, while ginger cream reduces inflammation and promotes digestion. Chamomile cream calms the nervous system, reducing stress and anxiety that can exacerbate stomach cramps. Applying a combination of these creams to the abdomen can provide relief from stomach cramps and promote overall digestive health. This blend can be used individually or in combination for effective relief.
